prompt: Toque el botón "información" para obtener más información
answer: Toque el botón "información" para obtener más información

info:
Spanish letters are mostly pronounced similar to their English counterparts, although there are some differences.
Spanish spelling → pronunciation is consistent; English is not.
Spanish vowels are pure and fully pronounced, even at the end of words.
Every syllable counts — no lazy reduction like English “probly”.
Stress is predictable and strongly affects how endings sound.
Keep that in mind as you read the letters.

Focus on pronuncation
Tap the "info" button now and for each letter for more info

Spanish a = open, steady /a/

English “a” slides (cat, cake, about)

Key difference: Spanish a never changes and is always fully pronounced.

B / V

Different (merged in Spanish)

Spanish b and v sound the same

Between vowels they soften to a light /β/ (almost like a soft b)

In Spanish, C is soft (/s/ or /θ/) only when it comes before E or I.
So any Spanish word starting with:
ce-, ci-.  will have a soft C sound.

Examples (word-initial)
cena (dinner)
cielo (sky)

Pronunciation note (important)
In Latin America: soft C = /s/
In Spain: soft C = /θ/ (the “th” in thin)

There are no Spanish words starting with ca-, co-, cu- that have a soft C.

casa, cosa → always hard /k/

Spanish q is just /k/.
No /w/
No lip rounding
No extra glide
Spanish uses q only to preserve spelling rules:
c + e/i would be soft (/s/ or /θ/)
To force a hard /k/ before e/i, Spanish writes qu

What the Spanish trill r actually is
The trilled R (IPA /r/) is not an English r
It is rapid, repeated taps of the tongue tip against the alveolar ridge (the bumpy area just behind your top teeth), caused by airflow, not muscular flapping.
Think: air makes the tongue vibrate — you don’t wag it.

When Spanish uses the trill:
Spanish uses two R sounds:

R position    -    Sound
Single r between vowels   -   Tap /ɾ/ (pero)
rr between vowels   -   Trill /r/ (perro)
Word-initial r   -   Trill /r/ (rojo)
r after l, n, s   -   Trill /r/ (alrededor, Enrique, Israel)

So:
caro = tap (expensive)
carro = trill (car)
Miss this distinction and you change meaning.

The physical setup:
Place your tongue tip lightly on the alveolar ridge
(same place as t or d, but relaxed)
Keep the tongue loose, not pressed
Exhale steadily
Let the tongue vibrate on its own
If you feel tension, stop — tension prevents trilling.

What it should feel like (important):
The tongue buzzes
You are not controlling each hit
Air pressure does the work
The sound is clean and fast, not dramatic
A good trill is shorter than learners expect.

S
The /s/ is one of the biggest regional markers in Spanish, especially in the Caribbean.

The “neutral / standard” Spanish S
In careful or “textbook” Spanish:
S = always /s/
Never becomes /z/ like English
Clearly pronounced at the end of syllables and words

In Cuban, Puerto Rican, Dominican, and much of coastal Venezuela/Colombia, syllable-final /s/ often weakens.

There are three common outcomes, in order of strength:
1. Full /s/ (least common in casual speech)
Usually in careful or emphatic speech

2. Aspirated /h/
The most common form. Sounds like a light English h

Examples:
los amigos → loh amigos
estás aquí → ehtá aquí

3. Complete deletion
Very common in fast, casual speech
los dos → lo do
estamos → etamo

The S is weakened only in certain positions:
más → má
pescado → pehcado / pecao
los niños → lo niño
saber, casa, así → S stays clear

The subtle thing English speakers miss most
Spanish does not weaken word endings
-ado, -ido, -ente, -ción → all clearly pronounced
English reduces: wanted → want-əd
Spanish does not do this. Every vowel survives.

listen to
-ado → hablado (“spoken”)
-ido → vivido (“lived”)
-ente → presente (“present”)
-ción → información (“information”)
prompt: Spanish does not weaken word endings.  hablado. vivido. presente. información.
answer: Spanish does not weaken word endings.  hablado. vivido. presente. información.

info:
Tap R /ɾ/ (not just the trill)
Many focus on the trill, but the tap causes more errors.
Sounds like a very fast d
This sound exists in English only accidentally (the “tt” in butter), so people don’t notice it.
prompt: Tap R /ɾ/ (not just the trill).  pero. cara. mira.
answer: Tap R /ɾ/ (not just the trill).  pero. cara. mira.

info:
The soft B/V (/β/)
This one is huge and almost never taught well.
Between vowels: b/v ≈ soft friction sound
Lips barely touch; no full stop
English speakers make it a hard b or v, which sounds foreign but still understandable.

listen to:
saber
la vida
haber
prompt:
The soft B/V (/β/). 
This one is huge and almost never taught well.
Between vowels: b/v ≈ soft friction sound
Lips barely touch; no full stop
English speakers make it a hard b or v, which sounds foreign but still understandable.
answer:
The soft B/V (/β/). 
This one is huge and almost never taught well.
Between vowels: b/v ≈ soft friction sound
Lips barely touch; no full stop
English speakers make it a hard b or v, which sounds foreign but still understandable.

info:
The soft D (/ð/)

Another quiet one.
d between vowels = th in this
Not as strong as English th
This is part of why -do endings sound “lighter” in native speech without disappearing.

Examples:
cada
nada
hablado
prompt: The soft D (/ð/).  cada. nada. hablado.
answer: The soft D (/ð/).  cada. nada. hablado.

info:
The Spanish G between vowels (/ɣ/)

This is the sibling of the soft B/D.
Between vowels: g becomes a soft friction sound
Not a hard stop
English speakers usually pronounce a full g, which stiffens the word.
prompt: The Spanish G between vowels (/ɣ/).  lago. amigo. llegar.
answer: The Spanish G between vowels (/ɣ/).  lago. amigo. llegar.

info:
Ñ (/ɲ/)

people underestimate how tight it is.
Not “n + y” . Single palatal sound

Compare:
año
anio (different word!)

LL / Y (/ʝ/ family)

This one varies by region, but it’s not English-like anywhere.
Depending on dialect:
Soft y
zh (like vision)
sh
English speakers default to y, which is often too weak.
prompt: LL / Y (/ʝ/ family).  llamar. calle. ayer
answer: LL / Y (/ʝ/ family).  llamar. calle. ayer

info:
The Spanish J / G (ge, gi) (/x/)

Throaty, but not a growl
Produced further forward than Arabic or Hebrew
English has no equivalent.
prompt: The Spanish J / G (ge, gi) (/x/).  jugar. gente. trabajo
answer: The Spanish J / G (ge, gi) (/x/).  jugar. gente. trabajo

info:
Unaspirated stops (P, T, K)
These are subtle but audible.
Spanish p/t/k lack the puff of air English has
Especially noticeable at word starts
English speakers blow too much air.

Try:
papa
taco
casa
prompt: Unaspirated stops (P, T, K).  papa. taco. casa
answer: Unaspirated stops (P, T, K).  papa. taco. casa

info:
Rhythm
Spanish is syllable-timed.

Each syllable:
Gets time
Gets a vowel
Gets clarity
English collapses unstressed syllables.
This is why Spanish can sound “machine-gun fast” but is actually very even.

In Caribbean Spanish (Cuba, Puerto Rico, coastal Venezuela, etc.), there are specific, predictable reductions in casual speech.
The key one here is:
-ado → -ao
nublado → nublao
cansado → cansao
mojado → mojao
prompt: predictable reductions in casual speech.  nublado. cansado. mojado
answer: predictable reductions in casual speech.  nublado. cansado. mojado
